title: DIY Octagon Picnic Table
summary: A picnic table built on a center X of 2x4s with eight radiating spokes — every tabletop and seat board cut at 22.5°, every framing spoke at 30° and 45°, for a true eight-sided table.
description:
This table's whole structure is one big X, built first on a flat, level surface — the two long center 2x4s cross and pocket-screw together at the middle, and everything else in the frame radiates outward from that single joint. Getting that center X square and flat matters more than any other single step, since every spoke and every tabletop board's angle is referenced from it.

Once the X is down, the remaining framing spokes fill in around it at 22-1/2° between each "spoke" — pocket holes or metal brackets both work. Lay out every tabletop board before screwing any of them down permanently; boards vary in actual width board to board, and the plan explicitly recommends cutting the seat boards as you go rather than trusting the cut list numbers for them.

One structural tip from the source is worth following exactly: rotate the full-length seat support 90° from the full-length tabletop support, rather than stacking them in the same orientation — that cross-orientation is what gives the underside its real strength. If leg room feels tight once it's built, a documented reader modification extends the seat-support spokes 4" all the way around and moves the innermost seat board to the outside edge instead — a legitimate variant on the same base structure, not a different table.

tools.note: The center X must be built on a flat, level surface.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Tabletop and seat boards — cut as you go, boards vary in actual width.

materials.name: Self-tapping exterior screws, 2-1/2" to 3", star drive

cutList.note: Both ends tapered at 30° off square, not parallel to each other.
One end at 30° off square, other end pointed at 45° off square.
Both ends at 30° off square, parallel to each other.
Both ends tapered in at 30° off square. Rotate 90° from the tabletop's full-length spoke for cross-strength.
One end at 30° off square, other end pointed at 45° off square.
Both ends at 22.5° off square. Measure and cut as you go.
Both ends at 30° off square, parallel to each other. Measure and cut as you go.

steps.title: Cut the framing and the boards
steps.body:
Every framing spoke is 2x4 and every top and seat board is 2x6. Cut the table framing first: one 62" center spoke with both ends tapered 30° off square and not parallel to each other, two 30-1/2" half spokes with one end at 30°, and four 29-15/16" point spokes with one end at 30° and the other pointed at 45°. Then the seat framing the same way: one 95" center spoke, two 46-3/4" half spokes and four 46-7/16" point spokes. Seven of the ten 8 ft 2x4s cover all of that, but the 95" seat spoke leaves only about an inch on its board, so pick the straightest, check-free board for it and cut it first.

The tabletop is eight each of six lengths — 4-9/16", 9-1/2", 14-7/16", 19-5/16" and 24-1/4" all with both ends at 22-1/2° off square, and 32-15/16" with both ends at 30° and parallel. The seats are eight at 31-13/16" with 22-1/2° ends and eight at 36-3/4" with parallel 30° ends. Those 64 pieces use all fifteen listed 2x6s with well under an inch of slack on the tightest board, so buy a spare if you intend to follow the plan's advice and fit the seat boards by measurement as you go.

steps.title: Build the center X
steps.body: On a genuinely flat, level surface, lay the 62" table center spoke down and butt the two 30-1/2" half spokes into its sides to form the cross. Check the arithmetic before you fasten anything: 30-1/2" plus the 1-1/2" thickness of the center spoke plus 30-1/2" is 62-1/2", half an inch longer than the 62" center spoke — the seat framing's equivalent numbers close exactly, so trim the table half spokes to your actual spoke rather than forcing the joint. Join with pocket screws and glue; metal straps or plates work as well, but only on the underside where they cannot foul the tabletop boards. This one joint sets the reference for every other angle on the table, so check it for square and for flat before you go on.

steps.title: Add the remaining table framing spokes
steps.body: Add the four 29-15/16" point spokes so that all eight arms sit 22-1/2° apart around the center — that is 45° between each point spoke and the cross arm beside it. Each point spoke lands with its 30° end against the side of an existing spoke and its 45° point outward, where two adjacent tabletop boards will meet. Fasten with pocket holes or metal brackets. Measure from the center to each arm tip as you work: if the eight radii are not equal the octagon will not close, and you will discover that when the last tabletop board does not fit.

steps.title: Lay out and attach the tabletop
steps.body: Lay out all the tabletop boards first and adjust as necessary so they meet in the center of the spokes. Screw down with two predrilled screws per board.
materials: Self-tapping exterior screws, 2-1/2" to 3", star drive

steps.title: Build the seat framing
steps.body: Flip the table over and mark your leg positions before anything else goes on — and know going in that this plan contains no leg parts at all: no cut-list row, no length, no angle, and no stated table or seat height, so the legs are yours to work out from the three spare 2x4s. Start with the 95" seat center spoke laid across the underside rotated 90° from the tabletop's 62" full-length spoke; that cross-orientation is what gives the underside its real strength, and stacking the two the same way throws it away. Butt the two 46-3/4" half spokes into its sides — 46-3/4 plus 1-1/2 plus 46-3/4 is exactly 95", so this cross does close on its numbers — then add the four 46-7/16" point spokes the same way the tabletop framing went together. If leg room feels tight afterwards, the documented reader variant runs every seat spoke 4" longer and moves the innermost seat board out to the edge.

steps.title: Attach the seat boards
steps.body: Flip the table back over and screw down the seat boards, working from the outside in this time. Measure and cut each seat board as you go rather than trusting the cut list exactly, since board widths vary.
materials: Self-tapping exterior screws, 2-1/2" to 3", star drive

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the whole table and apply an exterior finish rated for fences or other outdoor lumber.

images.alt: Octagon picnic table built on an X-shaped center frame
